quiz_questions.question_text: Bar graphs are used for which type of data?
quiz_questions.explanation: Bar graphs display frequencies or counts of categorical data with separated bars.

quiz_questions.question_text: Histograms are used for which type of data?
quiz_questions.explanation: Histograms display the distribution of quantitative data by grouping values into bins.

quiz_questions.question_text: A key visual difference between bar graphs and histograms is:

options.text: Histograms use circles instead of bars
Bar graph bars touch; histogram bars have gaps
Histogram bars touch; bar graph bars have gaps

quiz_questions.explanation: Histogram bars are adjacent (touching) because they represent continuous intervals, while bar graph bars have gaps because categories are distinct.

quiz_questions.question_text: The y-axis of a histogram typically represents:
quiz_questions.explanation: The y-axis of a histogram shows how many data values fall within each bin (frequency or count).

quiz_questions.question_text: A histogram that is taller on the left and tapers to the right is described as:
quiz_questions.explanation: When the tail extends to the right (toward higher values), the distribution is right-skewed (positively skewed).

quiz_questions.question_text: The bars in a bar graph can be reordered without changing the meaning because:

options.text: Categories have no inherent numerical order
Bar graphs must always be alphabetical

quiz_questions.explanation: Since categorical data has no natural numerical ordering, the bars can be rearranged without altering the information conveyed.

quiz_questions.question_text: Changing the bin width in a histogram can:

options.text: Have no effect on the graph
Convert the histogram into a bar graph
Change the apparent shape of the distribution

quiz_questions.explanation: Different bin widths can reveal or hide features of the distribution, so choosing an appropriate bin width is important.

quiz_questions.question_text: A symmetric histogram has:

options.text: A long tail to the left
All bars of the same height
A long tail to the right
Roughly equal shapes on both sides of the center

quiz_questions.explanation: A symmetric distribution mirrors itself around the center, with both sides having approximately the same shape.

quiz_questions.question_text: A frequency histogram where each bar represents exactly one value (e.g., number of siblings: 0, 1, 2, 3) is used for:
quiz_questions.explanation: When each bar represents a single countable value, the histogram displays discrete quantitative data.

quiz_questions.question_text: A uniform distribution in a histogram means:

options.text: All bars have approximately the same height
The bars form a bell shape
One bar is much taller than the rest

quiz_questions.explanation: In a uniform distribution, data is spread evenly across all values or intervals, resulting in bars of roughly equal height.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a histogram?

options.text: A plot of individual data points
A graph with separated bars showing categorical data
A circular graph divided into slices
A graph with adjacent bars showing the frequency distribution of quantitative data grouped into intervals

quiz_questions.explanation: A histogram uses touching bars to show how quantitative data is distributed across bins (intervals), revealing the shape and spread of the data.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a bar graph?

options.text: A graph showing the relationship between two variables
A graph with separated bars showing the frequency or count of distinct categories
A graph with adjacent bars for continuous data intervals
A line connecting data points over time

quiz_questions.explanation: A bar graph uses separated bars to display the frequency, count, or other measure for each distinct category of qualitative data.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'skewed right' mean for a distribution?

options.text: The distribution has a longer tail extending toward lower values on the left
The distribution has a longer tail extending toward higher values on the right

quiz_questions.explanation: A right-skewed (positively skewed) distribution has most data concentrated on the left with a long tail stretching toward the higher values on the right.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'skewed left' mean for a distribution?

options.text: The distribution has a longer tail extending toward higher values
The distribution has a longer tail extending toward lower values on the left

quiz_questions.explanation: A left-skewed (negatively skewed) distribution has most data concentrated on the right with a long tail stretching toward the lower values on the left.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'bin' mean in the context of histograms?

options.text: The tallest bar in the histogram
An interval of values that defines one bar of the histogram

quiz_questions.explanation: A bin (or class interval) is a range of values that groups data together into a single bar of the histogram.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'bimodal distribution' mean?

options.text: A distribution that is perfectly flat
A distribution with two distinct peaks or modes

quiz_questions.explanation: A bimodal distribution has two noticeable peaks, suggesting the data may come from two different groups or processes.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'unimodal distribution' mean?

options.text: A distribution with exactly one peak or mode
A distribution that is always symmetric
A distribution with no clear peak

quiz_questions.explanation: A unimodal distribution has a single clear peak, meaning one value or interval has the highest frequency.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'relative frequency histogram' mean?

options.text: A histogram where the y-axis shows raw counts
A histogram where the y-axis shows proportions or percentages instead of counts
A bar graph with gaps between bars

quiz_questions.explanation: A relative frequency histogram has bars whose heights represent the proportion (or percentage) of the total data in each bin.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'Pareto chart'?

options.text: A bar graph where bars are arranged in order of decreasing frequency
A scatter plot with a trend line

quiz_questions.explanation: A Pareto chart is a special bar graph that arranges categories from most frequent to least frequent, often including a cumulative line.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'bell-shaped distribution' mean?

options.text: A distribution shaped like a triangle
A symmetric, unimodal distribution that resembles a bell, with data concentrated in the center and tapering equally on both sides

quiz_questions.explanation: A bell-shaped distribution is symmetric and unimodal, with most values clustered near the center and frequencies decreasing equally toward both tails.

quiz_questions.question_text: A teacher records test scores for 40 students: most score between 70-89, a few score in the 50-69 range, and a handful score 90-100. The histogram of these scores would most likely be:

options.text: Slightly skewed left with a peak in the 70-89 range

quiz_questions.explanation: With most scores in 70-89, a few lower scores, and some high scores, the distribution peaks at 70-89 with a slight left tail, making it left-skewed.

quiz_questions.question_text: A survey of 500 people asks their favorite pizza topping. Results: Pepperoni (180), Cheese (120), Mushroom (80), Sausage (70), Other (50). The most appropriate graph to display this data is:
quiz_questions.explanation: Pizza toppings are categorical data, and bar graphs are the appropriate choice for displaying frequencies of categories.

quiz_questions.question_text: A researcher creates two histograms of the same income data: one with bins of $5,000 width and another with bins of $20,000 width. The $5,000 bins show a jagged, spiky pattern while the $20,000 bins show a smooth curve. What explains this difference?

options.text: Wider bins always give better information
Narrower bins show more detail but more noise; wider bins smooth out variation but may hide patterns

quiz_questions.explanation: Bin width affects how the distribution appears: narrow bins show fine detail but can appear noisy, while wide bins provide a smoother picture but may obscure important features.

quiz_questions.question_text: A company creates a bar graph showing quarterly sales. The bars show: Q1 = $2M, Q2 = $2.1M, Q3 = $2.05M, Q4 = $2.15M. However, the y-axis starts at $1.9M instead of $0. This graph would:

options.text: Have no effect on the reader's perception
Visually exaggerate the differences between quarters
Make all bars look the same height

quiz_questions.explanation: Starting the y-axis at $1.9M instead of $0 magnifies relatively small differences, making the sales changes look more dramatic than they actually are.

quiz_questions.question_text: An analyst has data on the ages of 1,000 marathon runners. The histogram shows a peak around ages 30-35, with the distribution tapering off gradually toward older ages but dropping sharply below age 20. This distribution is:

options.text: Skewed right, because the tail extends toward older ages

quiz_questions.explanation: Since the distribution peaks at 30-35 and has a longer tail toward older ages (higher values), it is right-skewed.

quiz_questions.question_text: A city collects data on commute times. The histogram shows two distinct peaks: one around 15 minutes and another around 45 minutes. This distribution is best described as:
quiz_questions.explanation: Two distinct peaks indicate a bimodal distribution, possibly representing two groups: those who live close to work and those who live farther away.

quiz_questions.question_text: A school counselor creates a bar graph showing the number of students in each extracurricular activity. She arranges the bars from tallest to shortest. This arrangement is called a:
quiz_questions.explanation: A Pareto chart arranges categorical bars in descending order of frequency, making it easy to identify the most common categories.

quiz_questions.question_text: A hospital administrator wants to compare the number of patients admitted for five different conditions (heart disease, diabetes, respiratory illness, fractures, infections). She should use:

options.text: A bar graph, because the conditions are categories
A scatterplot, to show the relationship between conditions
A histogram, because the data is quantitative

quiz_questions.explanation: Medical conditions are categorical data, so a bar graph is the appropriate choice for comparing their frequencies.

quiz_questions.question_text: A real estate agent creates a histogram of home prices in a neighborhood. Most homes are priced between $200,000 and $400,000, but there are a few mansions priced over $1,000,000. The distribution shape would be:

options.text: Skewed right due to the expensive outliers pulling the tail to the right

quiz_questions.explanation: A few very expensive homes create a long right tail, making the distribution right-skewed (positively skewed).

quiz_questions.question_text: Two classes take the same exam. Class A's histogram shows a bell-shaped curve centered at 75. Class B's histogram shows a left-skewed distribution with most scores near 90. Which statement is correct?

options.text: The shape of the distribution has nothing to do with performance
Class B generally performed better, with most students scoring near 90, but a few low scores pull the tail left
Class A performed better because bell-shaped is always superior

quiz_questions.explanation: Class B's left-skewed distribution with a peak near 90 indicates most students scored high, with a few low scores creating the left tail.
